myth: You can't get pregnant the first time you have sex.
fact: You can get pregnant any time you have unprotected sex, even the very first time. Using contraception and condoms is essential for prevention.
Most contraceptives are reversible and do not cause long-term infertility. Once you stop using them, your fertility returns to its natural state.

myth: Only women need to worry about SRHR.
fact: SRHR is for everyone. Men need to understand and participate in reproductive health, including STI prevention and supporting partners.

myth: You can tell if someone has an STI just by looking at them.
fact: A lot of STIs, like HIV and Chlamydia, often show no visible symptoms. The only way to know for sure is to get tested.

myth: Standing up or washing after sex prevents pregnancy.
fact: Washing or standing up doesn't stop sperm from reaching an egg. Only reliable contraceptives or barriers like condoms work.

myth: People with disabilities aren't interested in sex.
fact: Sexuality is a natural part of human life for everyone, regardless of physical or cognitive ability.

myth: Abortion is always illegal in Kenya.
fact: The Kenyan constitution allows for abortion in specific cases, like when the health or life of the mother is at risk or in emergencies.

myth: Natural methods like withdrawal are 100% effective.
fact: Methods like withdrawal have high failure rates. Using modern contraceptives is much more reliable for preventing pregnancy.

myth: Periods are dirty or a curse.
fact: Menstruation is a healthy, natural biological process and not a sign of being 'dirty' or 'unclean'.

myth: Young people shouldn't talk about sex.
fact: Open and honest talk helps young people make safer choices and build healthier relationships based on respect and consent.

myth: Condoms are only for people with many partners.
fact: Condoms are for anyone who wants to prevent STIs and pregnancy, regardless of how many partners they have.
Anyone who is sexually active can get an STI. It has nothing to do with your character or morality.
SRHR also includes things like emotional well-being, healthy relationships, and making decisions about your future.

myth: Pregnancy is always safe and easy.
fact: Pregnancy can have complications. Regular checkups and prenatal care are important for the health of both mother and baby.

myth: Consent is only needed the first time.
fact: Consent is needed every single time you're intimate, even if you are in a long-term relationship or married.
